varieties.name: Standard Greater Yam
varieties.origin: Southeast Asia (likely Myanmar/Thailand), cultivated for thousands of years
varieties.traits: White to cream flesh, cylindrical tubers 15-30kg, smooth brown skin, vigorous climbing vine with winged stems
varieties.yield_notes: 25-40 t/ha, highly productive in one growing season
varieties.availability: Asian grocers, ethnic markets in Brisbane area. Available from community gardens (Northey Street City Farm, Brisbane)

varieties.name: Purple Yam (Ube)
varieties.origin: Native to Philippines, also Taiwan and Ryukyu Islands. Domesticated in Island Southeast Asia over 11,000 years ago
varieties.traits: Vivid purple to lavender flesh, irregular oval to cylindrical shape, thick bark-like skin, tubers 2-45kg
varieties.yield_notes: 20-35 t/ha, single large tuber per plant
varieties.availability: Filipino grocers, Asian markets. Purple yam plants increasingly available from specialty nurseries in Queensland. Seeds and cuttings from online suppliers.

varieties.name: Aerial Potato (Edible Form)
varieties.origin: Tropical Asia, Africa, Northern Australia. Naturalized in Far North Queensland
varieties.traits: Produces edible aerial tubers (bulbils) along stems 3-10cm diameter, plus underground tubers. Vigorous vine 5-10m per season. Sweet fragrance from summer flowers
varieties.yield_notes: Aerial bulbils harvestable throughout season, underground tubers less productive than winged yam but still substantial
varieties.availability: Community gardens, city farms (Northey Street City Farm, Brisbane). Shared at gardening open days. Some online nurseries.

varieties.name: Pencil Yam / Long Yam (Dioscorea transversa)
varieties.origin: Indigenous to eastern and northern Australia. Staple food of Australian Aboriginal peoples for thousands of years
varieties.traits: Slender, long cylindrical tubers (hence 'pencil yam'). Heart-shaped shiny leaves with 5-7 prominent veins. Rounded seed pods dry to papery straw brown texture, persist for months
varieties.availability: Indigenous nurseries, bushfood suppliers. University of Queensland research programs. Limited availability - conservation focus.

varieties.name: Native Warrain Yam (Dioscorea hastifolia)
varieties.origin: Endemic to Western Australia (not naturally occurring in Brisbane area, but included for reference)

varieties.name: Lesser Yam (Standard)
varieties.origin: Southeast Asia (likely Burma or Indo-China), cultivated for thousands of years. One of first yam species domesticated
varieties.traits: Smaller than greater yam. Vines seldom exceed 3m. Compact growth habit allows closer spacing. Multiple small tubers per plant
varieties.yield_notes: 15-25 t/ha, lower than greater yam but still productive
varieties.availability: Asian grocers, ethnic markets in Brisbane. Less common than greater yam but available seasonally.

varieties.name: Chinese Yam (Standard)
varieties.origin: East Asia (China, Korea, Japan). Cultivated for over 2000 years. Introduced to USA in 1800s, Europe in 1800s
varieties.traits: Spindle-shaped or cylindrical tubers up to 1m long, weighing up to 4.5kg. More frost-tolerant than other yams. Produces abundant aerial bulbils 4cm long. Cinnamon-scented flowers
varieties.yield_notes: High-yielding, comparable to potato fields per acre
varieties.availability: Asian markets (Korean, Japanese, Chinese grocers). Fresh tubers sometimes available. Specialty nurseries stock plants.

varieties.name: White Yam (Standard)
varieties.origin: Native to West Africa (Nigeria, Ghana, Benin, Ivory Coast). Independently domesticated in Africa. Introduced to Asia and Americas
varieties.traits: Cylindrical tubers, smooth brown skin, white firm flesh. Tubers typically 2-10kg, can reach 25kg. Vines 10-12m long, may have small spines at leaf axils
varieties.yield_notes: 30-40 t/ha in optimal conditions, single large tuber per plant
varieties.availability: African grocers, international markets in Brisbane. Increasingly available as Brisbane's African community grows.

varieties.name: Yellow Yam (Standard)
varieties.origin: Native to West Africa (Guinea, Senegal to Nigeria). Now cultivated in Caribbean and tropical Americas
varieties.traits: Similar appearance to white yam but yellow flesh from carotenoids. Tuber skin less grooved, firmer. Longer vegetative period, shorter dormancy than white yam

varieties.name: Cush-Cush Yam
varieties.origin: Native to Guyana region of South America. Only domesticated yam from New World. Important in Caribbean
varieties.traits: Growth cycle less related to seasonal changes (originated in tropical rainforest). Multiple small to medium tubers. Compact growth
varieties.yield_notes: Moderate, produces crop after only 4 months and continues for up to 2 years
varieties.availability: Caribbean grocers, South American food markets in Brisbane. Rare but worth seeking.

varieties.name: Oca Varieties
varieties.origin: Andean highlands of South America. Second most important root crop after potato in Andes
varieties.traits: Small tubers 3-10cm, crinkled waxy appearance. Various colors (red, pink, cream, yellow). Plants sensitive to heat >28°C
varieties.availability: Daley's Fruit in Queensland stocks multiple Oca varieties. New Zealand seed potato suppliers.

varieties.name: Sree Keerthi
varieties.origin: Developed by Central Tuber Crops Research Institute (CTCRI), Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala
varieties.traits: Conical tubers with brown skin and white flesh. Compact growth habit suitable for intercropping

varieties.name: Sree Roopa
varieties.origin: CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala. Selected from germplasm collection
varieties.traits: Digitate (finger-like) shaped tubers with black/brownish-black skin and white flesh. More irregular shape than Sree Keerthi

varieties.name: Sree Shilpa
varieties.origin: CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala
varieties.traits: Swollen, oval, smooth tubers with black skin and white flesh. More uniform shape than Sree Roopa
varieties.yield_notes: 28 t/ha in 8 months - SHORTER growing season than other varieties

varieties.name: Indu
varieties.origin: Developed by Kerala Agricultural University (KAU)
varieties.traits: Digitate tubers with brownish-black skin and white to pale flesh

varieties.name: Sree Karthika
varieties.origin: CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala
varieties.traits: Pale green colored leaves (distinctive identification feature)
varieties.availability: CTCRI, Kerala agricultural extension network

varieties.name: CO 1
varieties.origin: Developed in Tamil Nadu, widely grown in Kerala
varieties.traits: Standard greater yam characteristics

varieties.name: Sree Latha
varieties.origin: Selection from Thiruvananthapuram district by CTCRI
varieties.traits: Oblong to fusiform tubers with creamy white flesh. Vines twine to the left (identifying feature). Medium-sized tubers 100-150g
varieties.availability: CTCRI, KAU, widely grown in Thiruvananthapuram and surrounding districts including Pathanamthitta (Thiruvalla)

varieties.name: Sree Kala
varieties.origin: CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala

varieties.name: Sree Subhra
varieties.origin: Developed by CTCRI from introduced African germplasm
varieties.traits: Cylindrical tubers with brown, partially hairy skin and white flesh. Drought-tolerant
varieties.yield_notes: 35-40 t/ha in 9-10 months - HIGHER than greater yam
varieties.availability: CTCRI, KAU, expanding availability through Kerala agricultural network

varieties.name: Sree Priya
varieties.origin: CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram
varieties.traits: Produces 2-3 tubers per plant with smooth surface. Drought-tolerant
varieties.availability: CTCRI, KAU, increasingly popular in Kerala

varieties.name: Sree Dhanya
varieties.origin: CTCRI - first dwarf white yam variety
varieties.traits: DWARF and BUSH variety (unique!), spineless stems. Compact growth habit

varieties.name: Sree Padma
varieties.origin: Developed by CTCRI, Thiruvananthapuram
varieties.traits: Large corms, uniform shape
varieties.availability: CTCRI, KAU, very widely available throughout Kerala agricultural system. Popular in Thiruvalla region

varieties.name: Sree Athira
varieties.origin: CTCRI - first genetically improved elephant foot yam variety
varieties.traits: Genetically improved for enhanced traits
varieties.availability: CTCRI, KAU, Kerala agricultural extension network

varieties.name: Gajendra
varieties.origin: Developed by Andhra Pradesh Agricultural University (APAU), Hyderabad
varieties.traits: Standard elephant foot yam characteristics
varieties.availability: Available through Kerala agricultural departments, increasingly grown in Kerala

varieties.name: Purple Yam (General)
varieties.origin: Southeast Asia (Philippines, Taiwan), widely cultivated across tropical Asia
varieties.traits: Vivid purple to lavender flesh from anthocyanins, irregular shape, thick bark-like skin
varieties.availability: Asian markets, Indian grocers in Kerala. Filipino and Southeast Asian communities. Growing in popularity for health benefits.

varieties.name: Aerial Potato
varieties.origin: Native to tropical Africa, Asia, and Northern Australia. Widespread across India
varieties.traits: Produces aerial tubers (bulbils) and underground tubers. Very vigorous vine growth
varieties.availability: Grows wild in many areas of Kerala. Available from traditional farmers, not widely commercially cultivated

varieties.name: Chinese Yam
varieties.origin: East Asia (China, Korea, Japan)
varieties.availability: Chinese/Korean grocers in major Kerala cities. Limited cultivation in Kerala - more common in North India

varieties.name: Japanese Mountain Yam (Dioscorea japonica)
varieties.origin: Native to Japan
varieties.traits: Similar to Chinese yam but distinct species. Mucilaginous texture
varieties.availability: Rare in Kerala. Japanese community gardens may have specimens.

varieties.name: Cush-Cush Yam (Dioscorea trifida)
varieties.origin: Guyana region, South America. Only New World yam species
varieties.traits: Originated in tropical rainforest - growth cycle less seasonal
varieties.availability: Rare in India. Would need to source from Caribbean or South American diaspora communities. Worth importing for Kerala cultivation trials.

varieties.name: Yellow Yam (Dioscorea cayenensis)
varieties.origin: West Africa
varieties.traits: Yellow-orange flesh from carotenoids (high beta-carotene), requires high rainfall
varieties.availability: Rare in India. African community markets in major cities. Could be introduced through CTCRI germplasm collection.

varieties.name: Sree Rashmi
varieties.origin: Developed by Kerala Agricultural University
varieties.traits: Conical cormels, economic yield under low input levels
varieties.availability: KAU, widely available in Kerala

varieties.name: Sree Pallavi
varieties.origin: Kerala Agricultural University
varieties.traits: Club-shaped cormels, field tolerant to leaf blight and mosaic diseases

varieties.name: Sreekiran
varieties.origin: First HYBRID taro variety in India
varieties.traits: Long keeping quality of cormels - extended storage
